question:
Let $A$ be the $n \times n$ matrix whose entry in the $i$-th row and $j$-th column is
\[
\frac{1}{\min(i,j)}
\]
for $1 \leq i,j \leq n$. Compute $\det(A)$.
solution:
Let $v_1,\ldots,v_n$ denote the rows of $A$. The determinant is 
unchanged if we replace $v_n$ by $v_n-v_{n-1}$, and then $v_{n-1}$ by 
$v_{n-1}-v_{n-2}$, and so forth, eventually replacing $v_k$ by 
$v_k-v_{k-1}$ for $k\geq 2$. Since $v_{k-1}$ and $v_k$ agree in their 
first $k-1$ entries, and the $k$-th entry of $v_k-v_{k-1}$ is 
$\frac{1}{k} - \frac{1}{k-1}$, the result of these row operations is an upper triangular 
matrix with diagonal entries $1,\frac{1}{2}-1,\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{2},\ldots,\frac{1}{n}-\frac{1}{n-1}$. The 
determinant is then
\begin{align*}
\prod_{k=2}^n \left( \frac{1}{k} - \frac{1}{k-1}\right) &= \prod_{k=2}^n \left(\frac{-1}{k(k-1)} \right) \\
&= 
\frac{(-1)^{n-1}}{(n-1)!n!}.
\end{align*}
Note that a similar calculation can be made whenever $A$ has the form $A_{ij} = \min\{a_i, a_j\}$ for any monotone sequence $a_1,\dots,a_n$. Note also that the standard Gaussian elimination algorithm leads to the same upper triangular matrix, but the nonstandard order of operations used here makes the computations somewhat easier.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
The inverse of $A$ can be identified explicitly: for $n \geq 2$, it is the matrix $B$ given by
\[
B_{ij} = \begin{cases} -1 & i=j=1 \\
-2i^2 & 1 < i=j< n \\
-(n-1)n & i=j=n \\
ij & |i-j|=1 \\
0 & \mbox{otherwise.} \end{cases}
\]
For example, for $n=5$,
\[
B = \begin{pmatrix}
-1 & 2 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
2 & -8 & 6 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 6 & -18 & 12 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 12 & -32 & 20 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 20 & -20
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Let $C$ denote the matrix obtained from $B$ by replacing the bottom-right entry with $-2n^2$ (for consistency with the rest of the diagonal).
Expanding in minors along the bottom row produces a second-order recursion for $\det(C)$ solving to $\det(C) = (-1)^n (n!)^2$; a similar expansion then yields $\det(B) = (-1)^{n-1} n! (n-1)!$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
This problem and solution are due to one of us (Kedlaya). The statement appears in the comments on sequence A010790 (i.e., the sequence $(n-1)! n!$)
in the On-Line Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences (\url{http://oeis.org}),
attributed to Benoit Cloitre in 2002.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $matrixa$ be the $sizeparam \times sizeparam$ matrix whose entry in the $indexi$-th row and $indexj$-th column is
\[
\frac{1}{\min(indexi,indexj)}
\]
for $1 \leq indexi,indexj \leq sizeparam$. Compute $\det(matrixa)$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Let $rowvecone,\ldots,rowvecn$ denote the rows of $matrixa$. The determinant is 
unchanged if we replace $rowvecn$ by $rowvecn-rowvecnminusone$, and then $rowvecnminusone$ by 
$rowvecnminusone-v_{sizeparam-2}$, and so forth, eventually replacing $rowveck$ by 
$rowveck-rowveckminusone$ for $indexk\geq 2$. Since $rowveckminusone$ and $rowveck$ agree in their 
first $indexk-1$ entries, and the $indexk$-th entry of $rowveck-rowveckminusone$ is 
$\frac{1}{indexk} - \frac{1}{indexk-1}$, the result of these row operations is an upper triangular 
matrix with diagonal entries $1,\frac{1}{2}-1,\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{2},\ldots,\frac{1}{sizeparam}-\frac{1}{sizeparam-1}$. The 
determinant is then
\begin{align*}
\prod_{indexk=2}^{sizeparam} \left( \frac{1}{indexk} - \frac{1}{indexk-1}\right) &= \prod_{indexk=2}^{sizeparam} \left(\frac{-1}{indexk(indexk-1)} \right) \\
&= \frac{(-1)^{sizeparam-1}}{(sizeparam-1)!sizeparam!}.
\end{align*}
Note that a similar calculation can be made whenever $matrixa$ has the form $matrixaentry = \min\{\elemseqi, \elemseqj\}$ for any monotone sequence $a_1,\dots,a_{sizeparam}$. Note also that the standard Gaussian elimination algorithm leads to the same upper triangular matrix, but the nonstandard order of operations used here makes the computations somewhat easier.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
The inverse of $matrixa$ can be identified explicitly: for $sizeparam \geq 2$, it is the matrix $matrixb$ given by
\[
matrixbentry = \begin{cases} -1 & indexi=indexj=1 \\
-2\,indexi^2 & 1 < indexi=indexj< sizeparam \\
-(sizeparam-1)sizeparam & indexi=indexj=sizeparam \\
indexi\,indexj & |indexi-indexj|=1 \\
0 & \mbox{otherwise.} \end{cases}
\]
For example, for $sizeparam=5$,
\[
matrixb = \begin{pmatrix}
-1 & 2 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
2 & -8 & 6 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 6 & -18 & 12 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 12 & -32 & 20 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 20 & -20
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Let $matrixc$ denote the matrix obtained from $matrixb$ by replacing the bottom-right entry with $-2\,sizeparam^2$ (for consistency with the rest of the diagonal).
Expanding in minors along the bottom row produces a second-order recursion for $\det(matrixc)$ solving to $\det(matrixc) = (-1)^{sizeparam} (sizeparam!)^2$; a similar expansion then yields $\det(matrixb) = (-1)^{sizeparam-1} sizeparam! (sizeparam-1)!$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
This problem and solution are due to one of us (Kedlaya). The statement appears in the comments on sequence A010790 (that is, the sequence $(sizeparam-1)! sizeparam!$)
in the On-Line Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences (\url{http://oeis.org}),
attributed to Benoit Cloitre in 2002.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $starflower$ be the $evergreen \times evergreen$ matrix whose entry in the $lighthouse$-th row and $horseshoe$-th column is
\[
\frac{1}{\min(lighthouse,horseshoe)}
\]
for $1 \leq lighthouse,horseshoe \leq evergreen$. Compute $\det(starflower)$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Let $blueberry,\ldots,blacksmith$ denote the rows of $starflower$. The determinant is 
unchanged if we replace $blacksmith$ by $blacksmith-bricklayer$, and then $bricklayer$ by 
$bricklayer-v_{evergreen-2}$, and so forth, eventually replacing $brainstorm$ by 
$brainstorm-but terfly$ for $dragonfly\geq 2$. Since butterfly and brainstorm agree in their 
first $dragonfly-1$ entries, and the $dragonfly$-th entry of $brainstorm-but terfly$ is 
$\frac{1}{dragonfly} - \frac{1}{dragonfly-1}$, the result of these row operations is an upper triangular 
matrix with diagonal entries $1,\frac{1}{2}-1,\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{2},\ldots,\frac{1}{evergreen}-\frac{1}{evergreen-1}$. The 
determinant is then
\begin{align*}
\prod_{dragonfly=2}^{evergreen} \left( \frac{1}{dragonfly} - \frac{1}{dragonfly-1}\right) &= \prod_{dragonfly=2}^{evergreen} \left(\frac{-1}{dragonfly(dragonfly-1)} \right) \\
&= 
\frac{(-1)^{evergreen-1}}{(evergreen-1)!evergreen!}.
\end{align*}
Note that a similar calculation can be made whenever $starflower$ has the form $riverstone = \min\{goldfinch, kingfisher\}$ for any monotone sequence $a_1,\dots,a_{evergreen}$. Note also that the standard Gaussian elimination algorithm leads to the same upper triangular matrix, but the nonstandard order of operations used here makes the computations somewhat easier.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
The inverse of $starflower$ can be identified explicitly: for $evergreen \geq 2$, it is the matrix $meadowlark$ given by
\[
thunderbolt = \begin{cases} -1 & lighthouse=horseshoe=1 \\
-2lighthouse^2 & 1 < lighthouse=horseshoe< evergreen \\
-(evergreen-1)evergreen & lighthouse=horseshoe=evergreen \\
lighthouse horseshoe & |lighthouse-horseshoe|=1 \\
0 & \mbox{otherwise.} \end{cases}
\]
For example, for $evergreen=5$,
\[
meadowlark = \begin{pmatrix}
-1 & 2 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
2 & -8 & 6 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 6 & -18 & 12 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 12 & -32 & 20 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 20 & -20
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Let $cloudberry$ denote the matrix obtained from $meadowlark$ by replacing the bottom-right entry with $-2evergreen^2$ (for consistency with the rest of the diagonal).
Expanding in minors along the bottom row produces a second-order recursion for $\det(cloudberry)$ solving to $\det(cloudberry) = (-1)^{evergreen} (evergreen!)^2$; a similar expansion then yields $\det(meadowlark) = (-1)^{evergreen-1} evergreen! (evergreen-1)!$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
This problem and solution are due to one of us (Kedlaya). The statement appears in the comments on sequence A010790 (i.e., the sequence $(evergreen-1)! evergreen!$)
in the On-Line Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences (\url{http://oeis.org}),
attributed to Benoit Cloitre in 2002.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $emptymatrix$ be the $trivialsize \times trivialsize$ matrix whose entry in the $aggregate$-th row and $fragment$-th column is
\[
\frac{1}{\min(aggregate,fragment)}
\]
for $1 \leq aggregate,fragment \leq trivialsize$. Compute $\det(emptymatrix)$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Let $scalarsolo,\ldots,vectorend$ denote the rows of $emptymatrix$. The determinant is 
unchanged if we replace $vectorend$ by $vectorend-vectoralmost$, and then $vectoralmost$ by 
$vectoralmost-v_{n-2}$, and so forth, eventually replacing $vectorcurrent$ by 
$vectorcurrent-vectorprev$ for $completed\geq 2$. Since $vectorprev$ and $vectorcurrent$ agree in their 
first $completed-1$ entries, and the $completed$-th entry of $vectorcurrent-vectorprev$ is 
$\frac{1}{completed} - \frac{1}{completed-1}$, the result of these row operations is an upper triangular 
matrix with diagonal entries $1,\frac{1}{2}-1,\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{2},\ldots,\frac{1}{trivialsize}-\frac{1}{trivialsize-1}$. The 
determinant is then
\begin{align*}
\prod_{completed=2}^{trivialsize} \left( \frac{1}{completed} - \frac{1}{completed-1}\right) &= \prod_{completed=2}^{trivialsize} \left(\frac{-1}{completed(completed-1)} \right) \
&= 
\frac{(-1)^{trivialsize-1}}{(trivialsize-1)!trivialsize!}.
\end{align*}
Note that a similar calculation can be made whenever $emptymatrix$ has the form $voidentry = \min\{steadyitem, steadyindex\}$ for any monotone sequence $steadyitem_1,\dots,steadyitem_{trivialsize}$. Note also that the standard Gaussian elimination algorithm leads to the same upper triangular matrix, but the nonstandard order of operations used here makes the computations somewhat easier.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
The inverse of $emptymatrix$ can be identified explicitly: for $trivialsize \geq 2$, it is the matrix $forwardmatrix$ given by
\[
forwardentry = \begin{cases} -1 & aggregate=fragment=1 \
-2aggregate^2 & 1 < aggregate=fragment< trivialsize \
-(trivialsize-1)trivialsize & aggregate=fragment=trivialsize \
aggregate fragment & |aggregate-fragment|=1 \
0 & \mbox{otherwise.} \end{cases}
\]
For example, for $trivialsize=5$,
\[
forwardmatrix = \begin{pmatrix}
-1 & 2 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
2 & -8 & 6 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 6 & -18 & 12 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 12 & -32 & 20 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 20 & -20
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Let $ordermatrix$ denote the matrix obtained from $forwardmatrix$ by replacing the bottom-right entry with $-2trivialsize^2$ (for consistency with the rest of the diagonal).
Expanding in minors along the bottom row produces a second-order recursion for $\det(ordermatrix)$ solving to $\det(ordermatrix) = (-1)^{trivialsize} (trivialsize!)^2$; a similar expansion then yields $\det(forwardmatrix) = (-1)^{trivialsize-1} trivialsize! (trivialsize-1)!$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
This problem and solution are due to one of us (Kedlaya). The statement appears in the comments on sequence A010790 (i.e., the sequence $(trivialsize-1)! trivialsize!$)
in the On-Line Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences (\url{http://oeis.org}),
attributed to Benoit Cloitre in 2002.

garbled_string.question:
Let $ldaepryt$ be the $lqdfxsnm \times lqdfxsnm$ matrix whose entry in the $dbxesrgo$-th row and $umqkzyle$-th column is
\[
\frac{1}{\min(dbxesrgo,umqkzyle)}
\]
for $1 \leq dbxesrgo,umqkzyle \leq lqdfxsnm$. Compute $\det(ldaepryt)$.
garbled_string.solution:
Let $tzhwmnqd,\ldots,hksrvadz$ denote the rows of $ldaepryt$. The determinant is 
unchanged if we replace $hksrvadz$ by $hksrvadz-bwxaymuc$, and then $bwxaymuc$ by 
$bwxaymuc-v_{n-2}$, and so forth, eventually replacing $qlopvtsa$ by 
$qlopvtsa-rjogafkt$ for $cfngirhv\geq 2$. Since $rjogafkt$ and $qlopvtsa$ agree in their 
first $cfngirhv-1$ entries, and the $cfngirhv$-th entry of $qlopvtsa-rjogafkt$ is 
$\frac{1}{cfngirhv} - \frac{1}{cfngirhv-1}$, the result of these row operations is an upper triangular 
matrix with diagonal entries $1,\frac{1}{2}-1,\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{2},\ldots,\frac{1}{lqdfxsnm}-\frac{1}{lqdfxsnm-1}$. The 
determinant is then
\begin{align*}
\prod_{cfngirhv=2}^{lqdfxsnm} \left( \frac{1}{cfngirhv} - \frac{1}{cfngirhv-1}\right) &= \prod_{cfngirhv=2}^{lqdfxsnm} \left(\frac{-1}{cfngirhv(cfngirhv-1)} \right) \\
&= \frac{(-1)^{lqdfxsnm-1}}{(lqdfxsnm-1)!lqdfxsnm!}.
\end{align*}
Note that a similar calculation can be made whenever $ldaepryt$ has the form $zjbxtqhe = \min\{gnzlkcwi, xchtavre\}$ for any monotone sequence $a_1,\dots,a_{lqdfxsnm}$. Note also that the standard Gaussian elimination algorithm leads to the same upper triangular matrix, but the nonstandard order of operations used here makes the computations somewhat easier.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
The inverse of $ldaepryt$ can be identified explicitly: for $lqdfxsnm \geq 2$, it is the matrix $oqsvdwmn$ given by
\[
ycnpfsar = \begin{cases} -1 & dbxesrgo=umqkzyle=1 \\
-2dbxesrgo^2 & 1 < dbxesrgo=umqkzyle< lqdfxsnm \\
-(lqdfxsnm-1)lqdfxsnm & dbxesrgo=umqkzyle=lqdfxsnm \\
dbxesrgoumqkzyle & |dbxesrgo-umqkzyle|=1 \\
0 & \mbox{otherwise.} \end{cases}
\]
For example, for $lqdfxsnm=5$,
\[
oqsvdwmn = \begin{pmatrix}
-1 & 2 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
2 & -8 & 6 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 6 & -18 & 12 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 12 & -32 & 20 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 20 & -20
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Let $kvourazi$ denote the matrix obtained from $oqsvdwmn$ by replacing the bottom-right entry with $-2lqdfxsnm^2$ (for consistency with the rest of the diagonal).
Expanding in minors along the bottom row produces a second-order recursion for $\det(kvourazi)$ solving to $\det(kvourazi) = (-1)^{lqdfxsnm} (lqdfxsnm!)^2$; a similar expansion then yields $\det(oqsvdwmn) = (-1)^{lqdfxsnm-1} lqdfxsnm! (lqdfxsnm-1)!$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark:}
This problem and solution are due to one of us (Kedlaya). The statement appears in the comments on sequence A010790 (i.e., the sequence $(n-1)! n!$)
in the On-Line Encyclopedia of Integer Sequences (\url{http://oeis.org}),
attributed to Benoit Cloitre in 2002.

kernel_variant.question:
Let n be a positive integer and put m = 2n.  
Form the m \times  m symmetric matrix  

  C = (c_{ij})_{1\leq i,j\leq m},  c_{ij} = min{i^2, j^2}.  

For the vector u = (1,2,\ldots ,m)^T and a scalar parameter \lambda  \in  \mathbb{C} consider the rank-one perturbation  

  A = C + \lambda  uu^T.  

(a)  Show that A is singular for exactly one value of \lambda , call it \lambda _n, and that A is invertible for every \lambda  \neq  \lambda _n.  
(b)  Compute det A in closed form as a function of n and \lambda .  
(c)  By normalising with det C, prove that det A / det C is an affine polynomial in \lambda  and determine its leading (hence signed) coefficient explicitly.
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0 Notation  
Throughout put  

  m = 2n  (n \geq  1),  H_k = 1 + \frac{1}{2} + \ldots  + 1/k (k-th harmonic number),  

and define  

  S_m := \Sigma _{k=1}^{m} 1/(2k-1) = H_{2m} - \frac{1}{2} H_m.  (1)

This last identity follows by splitting H_{2m} into the sum over odd and even indices.


Step 1 A structural factorisation of C  

Write L for the unit lower-triangular matrix  

  \ell _{ik} = 1   (if k \leq  i) ,  0  otherwise  (1 \leq  k,i \leq  m),

and P for the diagonal matrix  

  P = diag(2\cdot 1-1, 2\cdot 2-1, \ldots  , 2m-1) = diag(1,3,5,\ldots ,2m-1).

Because i^2 = \Sigma _{k=1}^{i}(2k-1), we have for every 1 \leq  i,j \leq  m  

 c_{ij} = min{i,j}^2 = \Sigma _{k=1}^{min{i,j}} (2k-1)
    = \Sigma _{k=1}^{m} (2k-1)\cdot 1_{k\leq i}\cdot 1_{k\leq j}
    = (L P L^T)_{ij}.  

Hence  

  C = L P L^T.  (2)

Both L and P are nonsingular, so C is positive-definite.


Step 2 The unperturbed determinant  

Because det L = 1 (unit triangular) and det L^T = 1, we obtain  

  det C = det P = \prod _{k=1}^{m} (2k-1).  (3)

Using \prod _{k=1}^{m} (2k) = 2^{m} m!,

  det C = (2m)! / (2^{m} m!) = (4n)! / (2^{2n}(2n)!).  (4)

For n=1 this gives det C = 3, agreeing with direct computation
det[[1,1],[1,4]] = 3.


Step 3 An explicit inverse of C  

From (2) we have C^{-1} = L^{-^T} P^{-1} L^{-1}.  
Because L is the matrix of cumulative sums, its inverse is the first-difference matrix:

  (L^{-1})_{ik} = {  1  if i = k,
                  -1 if i = k+1,
                   0 otherwise }  (5)

(i ranges vertically, k horizontally).  Consequently L^{-1}u is very simple:

  (L^{-1}u)_1 = u_1 = 1, and (L^{-1}u)_i = u_i - u_{i-1} = 1 (2 \leq  i \leq  m),

so  
  w := L^{-1}u = (1,1,\ldots ,1)^T.  (6)


Step 4 The quadratic form u^TC^{-1}u  

Using (6) and C^{-1} = L^{-^T} P^{-1} L^{-1},

 u^TC^{-1}u = w^T P^{-1} w
      = \Sigma _{k=1}^{m} 1\cdot 1 / (2k-1)
      = S_m.  (by (1))  (7)

Since every summand is positive, S_m > 0 for all m.


Step 5 The rank-one update  

For any invertible matrix C and vectors u,v the matrix-determinant lemma gives  
  det(C + \lambda  u v^T) = det C \cdot  (1 + \lambda  v^TC^{-1}u).  
Taking v = u and using (7),

  det A = det C \cdot  (1 + \lambda  S_m).  (8)

(a)  The parenthesis vanishes exactly when  

  \lambda  = \lambda _n := -1/S_m.  (9)

Hence A is singular precisely for this single value and nonsingular otherwise.

(b)  Equations (4) and (8) yield  

  det A = (4n)! / [2^{2n}(2n)!] \cdot  (1 + \lambda  S_{2n}).  (10)

(c)  Dividing (10) by det C from (4) removes the factorial factor:

  det A / det C = 1 + \lambda  S_{2n},  linear in \lambda ,  (11)
whose leading coefficient is  

  S_{2n} = H_{4n} - \frac{1}{2} H_{2n} > 0.  (12)

That coefficient grows like (\frac{1}{2}) log 2n + (\gamma /2) + log 2 as n\to \infty , so the
matrix becomes gradually more sensitive to the perturbation.


Step 6 Numerical check (n=1)  

m = 2 \Rightarrow  det C = 3, S_2 = 1 + 1/3 = 4/3, \lambda _1 = -\frac{3}{4}.  
Formula (10) gives det A = 3(1 + 4\lambda /3), which indeed vanishes at \lambda  = -\frac{3}{4}.

Hence every part is verified.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Additional parameter λ forces the solver to recognise and exploit the matrix-determinant lemma; the original problems required no perturbative analysis.  
•  Success now hinges on having the exact inverse of C, so the solver must either reproduce or import the banded inverse (tri-diagonal with quadratic entries) from the earlier remark and then evaluate a non-trivial quadratic form; this is substantially more work than the telescoping product in the original.  
•  Locating λₙ in part (a) introduces a divisor-of-zero phenomenon entirely absent from the previous statements.  
•  The algebraic manipulation of fourth–degree and second–degree power sums pushes the combinatorial side further than before, while rank-one updates bring linear-algebraic identities (determinant lemma, Sherman–Morrison) into play.  
•  Hence the problem demands familiarity with several distinct techniques—explicit inverses of special kernels, rank-one determinant updates, and power-sum evaluations—whereas the original task rested on a single clever row-operation cascade.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let n be a positive integer and put m = 2n.  
Form the m \times  m symmetric matrix  

  C = (c_{ij})_{1\leq i,j\leq m},  c_{ij} = min{i^2, j^2}.  

For the vector u = (1,2,\ldots ,m)^T and a scalar parameter \lambda  \in  \mathbb{C} consider the rank-one perturbation  

  A = C + \lambda  uu^T.  

(a)  Show that A is singular for exactly one value of \lambda , call it \lambda _n, and that A is invertible for every \lambda  \neq  \lambda _n.  
(b)  Compute det A in closed form as a function of n and \lambda .  
(c)  By normalising with det C, prove that det A / det C is an affine polynomial in \lambda  and determine its leading (hence signed) coefficient explicitly.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0 Notation  
Throughout put  

  m = 2n  (n \geq  1),  H_k = 1 + \frac{1}{2} + \ldots  + 1/k (k-th harmonic number),  

and define  

  S_m := \Sigma _{k=1}^{m} 1/(2k-1) = H_{2m} - \frac{1}{2} H_m.  (1)

This last identity follows by splitting H_{2m} into the sum over odd and even indices.


Step 1 A structural factorisation of C  

Write L for the unit lower-triangular matrix  

  \ell _{ik} = 1   (if k \leq  i) ,  0  otherwise  (1 \leq  k,i \leq  m),

and P for the diagonal matrix  

  P = diag(2\cdot 1-1, 2\cdot 2-1, \ldots  , 2m-1) = diag(1,3,5,\ldots ,2m-1).

Because i^2 = \Sigma _{k=1}^{i}(2k-1), we have for every 1 \leq  i,j \leq  m  

 c_{ij} = min{i,j}^2 = \Sigma _{k=1}^{min{i,j}} (2k-1)
    = \Sigma _{k=1}^{m} (2k-1)\cdot 1_{k\leq i}\cdot 1_{k\leq j}
    = (L P L^T)_{ij}.

Both L and P are nonsingular, so C is positive-definite.


Step 2 The unperturbed determinant  

Because det L = 1 (unit triangular) and det L^T = 1, we obtain  

  det C = det P = \prod _{k=1}^{m} (2k-1).  (3)

Using \prod _{k=1}^{m} (2k) = 2^{m} m!,

  det C = (2m)! / (2^{m} m!) = (4n)! / (2^{2n}(2n)!).  (4)

For n=1 this gives det C = 3, agreeing with direct computation
det[[1,1],[1,4]] = 3.


Step 3 An explicit inverse of C  

From (2) we have C^{-1} = L^{-^T} P^{-1} L^{-1}.  
Because L is the matrix of cumulative sums, its inverse is the first-difference matrix:

  (L^{-1})_{ik} = {  1  if i = k,
                  -1 if i = k+1,
                   0 otherwise }  (5)

(i ranges vertically, k horizontally).  Consequently L^{-1}u is very simple:

  (L^{-1}u)_1 = u_1 = 1, and (L^{-1}u)_i = u_i - u_{i-1} = 1 (2 \leq  i \leq  m),

so  
  w := L^{-1}u = (1,1,\ldots ,1)^T.  (6)


Step 4 The quadratic form u^TC^{-1}u  

Using (6) and C^{-1} = L^{-^T} P^{-1} L^{-1},

 u^TC^{-1}u = w^T P^{-1} w
      = \Sigma _{k=1}^{m} 1\cdot 1 / (2k-1)
      = S_m.  (by (1))  (7)

Since every summand is positive, S_m > 0 for all m.


Step 5 The rank-one update  

For any invertible matrix C and vectors u,v the matrix-determinant lemma gives  
  det(C + \lambda  u v^T) = det C \cdot  (1 + \lambda  v^TC^{-1}u).  
Taking v = u and using (7),

  det A = det C \cdot  (1 + \lambda  S_m).  (8)

(a)  The parenthesis vanishes exactly when  

  \lambda  = \lambda _n := -1/S_m.  (9)

Hence A is singular precisely for this single value and nonsingular otherwise.

(b)  Equations (4) and (8) yield  

  det A = (4n)! / [2^{2n}(2n)!] \cdot  (1 + \lambda  S_{2n}).  (10)

(c)  Dividing (10) by det C from (4) removes the factorial factor:

  det A / det C = 1 + \lambda  S_{2n},  linear in \lambda ,  (11)
whose leading coefficient is  

  S_{2n} = H_{4n} - \frac{1}{2} H_{2n} > 0.  (12)

That coefficient grows like (\frac{1}{2}) log 2n + (\gamma /2) + log 2 as n\to \infty , so the
matrix becomes gradually more sensitive to the perturbation.


Step 6 Numerical check (n=1)  

m = 2 \Rightarrow  det C = 3, S_2 = 1 + 1/3 = 4/3, \lambda _1 = -\frac{3}{4}.  
Formula (10) gives det A = 3(1 + 4\lambda /3), which indeed vanishes at \lambda  = -\frac{3}{4}.

Hence every part is verified.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Additional parameter λ forces the solver to recognise and exploit the matrix-determinant lemma; the original problems required no perturbative analysis.  
•  Success now hinges on having the exact inverse of C, so the solver must either reproduce or import the banded inverse (tri-diagonal with quadratic entries) from the earlier remark and then evaluate a non-trivial quadratic form; this is substantially more work than the telescoping product in the original.  
•  Locating λₙ in part (a) introduces a divisor-of-zero phenomenon entirely absent from the previous statements.  
•  The algebraic manipulation of fourth–degree and second–degree power sums pushes the combinatorial side further than before, while rank-one updates bring linear-algebraic identities (determinant lemma, Sherman–Morrison) into play.  
•  Hence the problem demands familiarity with several distinct techniques—explicit inverses of special kernels, rank-one determinant updates, and power-sum evaluations—whereas the original task rested on a single clever row-operation cascade.
